paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 13: The Spelling Bee (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 girl who represents her school at the National Inter-School Spelling Bee finals and loses on a word she actually knew, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The National Inter-School Spelling Bee finals were held at a hall in Bishan on a Saturday morning. I had taken the MRT there with my form teacher, Mr Tan. I was in Sec 2 and I was the only finalist from my school. I had been preparing for six months.

Paragraph 2
The first five rounds were easy. I spelled 'cinnamon', 'questionnaire', 'kaleidoscope', 'aquarium' and 'rendezvous'. After each correct answer the judge nodded, and I walked back to my seat trying not to smile too widely. I could see my parents in the third row.

Paragraph 3
In the sixth round the judge called my name and read out my word: 'fastidious'. I knew it. I had drilled it the night before. It meant being very careful about details — Mr Tan had taught me that. My heart jumped, but in a happy way. I opened my mouth to spell it.

Paragraph 4
Then I made a mistake. I did not wait for the judge to finish her usual second reading. I did not ask for the word in a sentence. I rushed. 'F-A-S-T-I-D-E-O-U-S,' I said quickly. The hall went quiet. 'I'm sorry,' the judge said gently. 'That is incorrect.' My ears began to ring.

Paragraph 5
I walked off the stage and sat down beside Mr Tan. He did not say anything at first. He passed me a small packet of tissues. I did not cry, but I held the tissues anyway. The competition continued on the stage. I heard the words being called but I could not really hear them.

Paragraph 6
On the bus home, I finally spoke. 'I knew that word,' I said. 'I really knew it.' Mr Tan nodded once. 'I should have slowed down,' I said. 'I should have asked for the sentence. I should have waited for the second reading.' He still did not say anything. The bus turned past the kopitiam at the end of my road.

Paragraph 7
'The hardest losses, Hui Lin,' Mr Tan said at last, 'are the ones you could have won.' He looked at me kindly. 'Now you know why your O-Levels will tell you the same thing one day. Knowing is not enough. You must also slow down. You must also check.' I stared at the back of the seat in front of me. I did not say anything. But I made myself remember every word.

Paragraph 8
That night, at my desk at home in Yishun, I wrote the word 'fastidious' on a small piece of paper. I spelled it correctly. I taped it to the edge of my study lamp where I would see it every evening. It is still there today.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past examination paper. MRT: Singapore's Mass Rapid Transit train system; kopitiam: a traditional coffee shop; O-Levels: the Singapore-Cambridge GCE Ordinary Level examinations.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, how long had the narrator been preparing for the finals? [1]
Q2. What does the word 'drilled' (Paragraph 3) suggest about how the narrator had learnt the word? [1]
questions.answer: she had practised it repeatedly / many times

questions.stem: Q3. Using your own words, explain why the narrator spelled the word wrongly even though she knew it. (Paragraph 4) [2]
questions.answer: ① she rushed and did not wait for the judge's second reading; ② she did not ask for the word in a sentence, so she answered carelessly

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the phrase 'My ears began to ring' (Paragraph 4)? [2]
questions.answer: it makes her shock physical and vivid — the moment hit her so hard that she could no longer hear the hall clearly

questions.stem: Q5. What does the word 'gently' (Paragraph 4) suggest about the way the judge spoke? [1]
questions.answer: she spoke kindly and softly, not harshly

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain what Mr Tan means when he says, 'The hardest losses are the ones you could have won.' (Paragraph 7) [2]
questions.answer: ① losing because of your own avoidable mistake hurts the most; ② it is harder to accept failure when you had the ability to win

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the narrator tapes the word to her study lamp where she will see it every evening. (Paragraph 8) [2]
questions.answer: ① she wants a daily reminder of the lesson she learnt; ② so that she will slow down and check, and never repeat the same mistake

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the morning of the spelling bee unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: confident / hopeful / devastated / wiser / bored / suspicious / angry / proud. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Spelling Bee' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the day unfolds — from confident as she finishes the first five rounds easily, to hopeful in the sixth round when she hears 'fastidious' and recognises a word she had drilled the night before, to devastated when she rushes the spelling and is told she is incorrect, ending wiser that night as she writes the word out correctly and tapes it to her study lamp. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2 as she finishes the first five rounds, spelling words like 'cinnamon' and 'rendezvous' correctly while her parents watch from the third row? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 when the judge reads out the word 'fastidious' and her heart jumps because she had drilled exactly that word the night before? [1]
Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4 after she rushes the answer, the judge says 'I'm sorry, that is incorrect,' and her ears begin to ring? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 that night at her desk in Yishun, writing 'fastidious' correctly on a small piece of paper and taping it to her study lamp where she will see it every evening? [1]
